items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.q: Which Miles Davis album popularised modal jazz?
items.why: It's Kind of Blue. “Bitches Brew” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which saxophonist is most associated with the development of bebop?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Charlie Parker. “John Coltrane” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' released?
items.why: The real figure is 1959. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
